# Extracao: transcription_mp4_06-_Course_4__Lesson_3__-_Key_Considerations___What_To_Look_For_In_A_Deal

---

Key considerations and what to look for in a deal. Look, if you take a step back, you'll see all this relates back to one thing, data. When you're investigating a potential deal or market, you need to collect all the data, the good, the bad, and yes, the ugly. When checking out a property, you should be looking for the reasons to buy, not the reasons not to buy. And remember, when you're looking at a property, you should be looking for the reasons to buy as much as you're looking for the reasons not to buy. This is what I call the balance between the optimist and the pessimist. You should be also looking for reasons other people would buy or wouldn't buy. And that's what you're going to use in your negotiations with yourself and the seller to determine can you pay a little extra or do you need to pay a little less? By the way, paying extra is not a bad thing. I have paid extra to get deals before. Deals that other people will pay me extra for in the future. Ultimately, you should focus on the four points before moving forward with a deal. Number one, you need to feel great about the place you're purchasing. There's tons of properties you may feel good about, properties you know you could make some money on and wind up possibly making some rate of return. But at the end of the day, you shouldn't move forward on some of these deals because the numbers just don't add up. You must feel good about a place before you move forward. You need to love it, not just like it. If it doesn't make you feel good, walk away. It's like people. Investing in properties is like people. If the people around you don't make you feel good, walk away. Go find something you love. This should be your number one criteria no matter what the numbers tell you. Number two, the personnel must be right at that property. You need to understand what kind of management is required to be in place now and in the future. By the way, the management that is there now when you're buying it may be great for now, but might not be able to take that property to the promised land, that place where you need it to be. You can blow up even the best investment property between their lack of engagement, shady dealings, and general blasé bad attitude or less than 10x attitude. You don't want to see that when you check out a building. Things like maintenance crews hanging around the lobby or workers smoking in the entrance or leasing agents that don't stand up when you crack the door to come in and chop the properties should be immediate red flags. Chances are, and by the way, there's plenty of that around, chances are there's lots lurking below the surface of that property that you're looking at that isn't going to make you or the tenants very happy. And that may inline the opportunity. Number three, the processes must be right. Same goes for processes. People in processes can negatively impact a property more than anything else. So when you're studying the financials and then you find out they have bad people and no processes, you know immediately a good operator can go in, improve the people, improve the processes, and then therefore improve the property itself and improve the cash flow. By process, keep them up, and this means everything from lease agreements, background checks, maintenance plans, and everything in between, they need to be on point. Answering the telephone, to handling tenant complaints, to bringing new tenants in, to turning over a property so that you can put a new tenant in. All that is going to make your property operate better and function better. I once bought a building and later found out that they'd stacked the building with not desirable tenants in the last 60 to 90 days as they prepared to sell it in the market to show a higher occupancy to fool the buyer. These tenants, we later found out by the way they did fool me, these tenants later we found out were felons, people with terrible credit, renters with zero plans to make payments and the like. It was less than ideal. I learned a lot from this. To say the least, and there was little I could do after I'd already taken over except clean the property up. I made a note of that. That's what that seller does when they sell a property. The next time I looked at a piece of property that that seller had, I knew he couldn't fool me again. Look, going in, you need to make sure everyone is background checked. Tenants, rent rolls, and the employees, and have them credit checked. Understand any freebies and concessions given. Concession is maybe free two weeks or four weeks or six weeks free. It's going to impact your financial statement. You need to know what's on paper syncs with who's in the building. If you don't or can't vouch, what does the military say? Trust but validate. You could be in serious trouble if you don't do this. You'll either be in trouble now or in the not too distant future. Number four, the product, the apartment building needs to have potential. You'll only make money investing in real estate if you have a cash flow or if your property appreciates are both. That way you can sell at a higher price. Your property, the location, must have potential for the future. You need that positive cash flow now as discussed and you need to give the building time to appreciate whether you force appreciation, we'll be talking about how to force appreciation or whether time just brings you that appreciation. To that end, look for properties that have potential. Focus on historic trends that can dictate future outcomes and values. Look at the local market trends, the rental price shifts. Look at the changes in the local population, are they positive or negative, that will impact how in demand your units. Look for people that are building next door or across the street or even in another part of town. You'll want to feel in your gut backed by data that your purchase, your investment, your building makes sense now and in the future.
